prompt_text: Best business intelligence platforms for build dashboards and reports without writing SQL
response:
For building dashboards and reports **without writing SQL**, the leading BI platforms are **Microsoft Power BI**, **Tableau**, **Qlik Sense**, **Looker Studio**, **Domo**, **ThoughtSpot**, and **Metabase**, all of which provide strong visual, drag‑and‑drop or natural‑language interfaces for non‑technical users.[1][2][3][4][5][6]

Below is a concise comparison focused on *no‑SQL, self‑service dashboarding*:

| Platform | Why it’s good for non‑SQL users | Typical use case / fit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Microsoft Power BI** | Drag‑and‑drop canvas, many prebuilt connectors, strong guided analytics; designed so “everyone…make confident decisions…with a **no‑code drag‑and‑drop interface**.”[1][2] | Best if you’re already on Microsoft 365 or Azure; great price‑to‑value for SMB to enterprise. |
| **Tableau** | Visual drag‑and‑drop for building charts and dashboards; users can stay entirely in the UI while data modelers handle more advanced logic.[2][6] | Data‑heavy orgs needing rich, polished visualizations and interactive dashboards. |
| **Qlik Sense** | Associative engine plus drag‑and‑drop; strong self‑service exploration without writing queries.[2][6] | When you need powerful filtering, in‑memory analytics, and governed self‑service BI. |
| **Looker Studio (ex‑Google Data Studio)** | Free, **fully customizable dashboards and reports**; intuitive UI, works “seamlessly” with Google Ads, YouTube, BigQuery, etc.[1] | Marketing/web analytics, small teams, and Google‑centric stacks; best free option. |
| **Domo** | Cloud BI with strong visual UX and self‑service dashboards aimed at business users.[4][6] | Executives and business teams needing cross‑department dashboards in the cloud. |
| **ThoughtSpot** | Search‑driven analytics; users type or click to ask questions and get visual answers, designed for non‑technical users.[5] | Line‑of‑business users who prefer a Google‑like search bar over a report builder. |
| **Metabase** | “Easy, open‑source BI tool everyone…can use” to ask questions and build dashboards; simple GUI question builder.[1][3] | Product/tech companies, startups, and teams wanting open‑source and low cost. |
| **Trevor.io / Visual SQL tools (e.g., Chartio‑style)** | **No‑code query builder** and “Visual SQL…allows anyone to query data without knowing how to code in SQL,” via drag‑and‑drop query building.[1][3] | When you want a visual query builder that still lets analysts go deeper when needed. |
| **Klipfolio** | Focused on making dashboards “easy to create and maintain…for people with no coding experience.”[3] | Real‑time KPI dashboards, especially for agencies and SaaS metrics. |
| **Cumul.io / Panintelligence** | Dashboard‑centric tools with drag‑and‑drop UI; Cumul.io lets “everyone build their own dashboards” and embed them into apps.[1] | If you need to **embed** dashboards in your own SaaS product with minimal code. |

### How to choose the right tool (no‑SQL focus)

When you mainly care about *not* writing SQL, prioritize:

- **Interface for non‑technical users**
  - Look for explicit **drag‑and‑drop** builders and “no‑code” or “visual query” language.[1][3]  
  - Power BI, Tableau, Qlik Sense, Looker Studio, Domo, ThoughtSpot and Metabase all emphasize self‑service exploration for business users.[1][2][3][4][5][6]

- **Connectors to your data sources**
  - If you use Google Ads/Analytics/BigQuery → **Looker Studio** is the smoothest.[1]  
  - Heavy Microsoft & SQL Server / Azure use → **Power BI** is usually best.[2][4][8]  
  - Mixed SaaS + databases → **Tableau**, **Domo**, **Qlik Sense**, **Metabase**, or **Klipfolio** work well.[2][3][4][6]

- **Governance and scale**
  - Larger orgs often pick **Power BI**, **Tableau**, **Qlik Sense**, or **Domo** for security, semantic layers, and admin controls.[2][4][5][6]  

- **Budget**
  - Free/low‑budget: **Looker Studio** (free), **Metabase** open‑source edition, plus low‑tier Power BI.[1][
